Missing_Entities: local game industry; Indonesia's creative economy; youth employment
Denser_Summary: This article discusses a study that examines how a particular fast-growing digital sector relates to a nation's creative economy and to job opportunities for younger workers. By way of a structured review of earlier scholarly publications, it weighs benefits, contributions, and difficulties in a general manner. The discussion centers on the local game industry, its role within Indonesia's creative economy, and its effect on youth employment, alongside broader economic and technological context the following summaries will progressively make more specific.

Missing_Entities: Systematic Literature Review (SLR); internet penetration; digital transformation
Denser_Summary: Indonesia's local game industry, a fast-growing part of its creative economy, is analyzed here for its economic role and its impact on youth employment. Applying a Systematic Literature Review (SLR) of the past decade's scholarship, the study synthesizes findings on contributions and obstacles. Rising internet penetration, mobile adoption, and digital transformation drive growth, positioning game development as a strategic creative subsector that generates high-value digital products and opens careers for young Indonesians, themes the remaining summaries specify and quantify further.

Missing_Entities: skills gap; foreign-game dominance; limited funding access
Denser_Summary: Indonesia's local game industry, a fast-growing creative-economy subsector, is examined via a Systematic Literature Review (SLR) spanning a decade for its economic role and youth-employment impact. Fueled by internet penetration, mobile adoption, and digital transformation, it yields high-value digital products and young careers. Yet it faces a persistent skills gap between education and industry, limited funding access, and domestic-market dominance by foreign games, which capture most revenue despite Indonesia's huge player base—challenges the later summaries connect to concrete solutions.

Missing_Entities: government–academia–industry collaboration; recurring income (in-app purchases); multiplier effect
Denser_Summary: Indonesia's local game industry, a fast-growing creative-economy subsector, is examined through a decade-spanning Systematic Literature Review for economic role and youth employment. Internet penetration, mobile adoption, and digital transformation drive high-value products, recurring income (in-app purchases, digital ads), and a multiplier effect across creative professions. It still confronts a skills gap, limited funding, and foreign-game dominance capturing most revenue. Stronger government–academia–industry collaboration is urged to build skills, fund local developers, and boost competitiveness—points the final summary anchors with evidence.

Missing_Entities: programmers, designers, animators, content creators; esports and mobile gaming labor; BPS 27.4 million creative workers (2025)
Denser_Summary: Indonesia's local game industry, a fast-growing creative-economy subsector, is reviewed (SLR, ten years) for economic role and youth employment. Internet penetration and digital transformation drive high-value products, recurring income, and a multiplier effect employing programmers, designers, animators, and content creators; esports and mobile gaming labor add flexible digital work. BPS reports creative industries employed 27.4 million people in 2025. Barriers—skills gap, limited funding, foreign-game dominance—demand government–academia–industry collaboration to fund developers, close skills gaps, and raise global competitiveness.
